name: Italian Game
description: One of the oldest openings in chess. White develops the bishop to c4, targeting the vulnerable f7 square and fighting for center control.

moves.commentary: Grab the center right away. This pawn opens lines for your bishop and queen — always a strong start.
They're matching you in the center. Good — this leads to an open, tactical game.
Bring your knight out and put pressure on their e5 pawn. Two jobs in one move.
They defend e5 with the knight. Standard stuff — now it's time for your bishop.
Point this bishop straight at f7 — that's their weakest spot. This is the Italian Game now.
They mirror you with their bishop on c5. It's eyeing your f2 — respect that.
Here's the plan: you're preparing d4 to build a big pawn center. 'Giuoco Piano' means 'quiet game' — but it's quietly dangerous.
They develop and counterattack your e4 pawn. Don't worry — push forward.
Now strike! This is what c3 was for. You want those pawns on d4 and e4 — that's a dream center.
They capture. That's fine — you planned for this.
Take back with the pawn. Look at that center — d4 and e4. You're in great shape.
They check you with the bishop. Stay calm — this is all theory.
Block with your bishop. After they trade, you'll still have that powerful center.
They trade off. Good — less pieces for them to attack you with.
Take back with the knight. Your pieces are active, your center is solid. You're ahead in development — nice work.
Center pawn first — you know the drill.
They match you. Italian Game territory incoming.
Knight out, attacking their pawn.
They defend. Time for the Italian bishop.
Aim at f7. Classic setup.
Their bishop comes out too. Now here's where it gets fun...
BOOM — the Evans Gambit! You're sacrificing a whole pawn to blast their bishop out of position. Bold chess.
They take the bait. That's a pawn gone, but watch what you get in return.
Chase that bishop away and get ready for d4. You're trading a pawn for a massive center and fast development.
They retreat but keep some pressure. No worries — your plan is working.
Here it is — the whole point. You've got a huge center and your pieces are flying out. That pawn was well spent.
They capture. The center opens up — exactly what you want with better development.
Castle first! King safety before material recovery. Your rook now eyes the center too.
They try to slow you down by blocking. Understandable — your position looks scary.
Queen comes out hitting f7 AND d3. You've got a powerful attack brewing — this is why people fear the Evans Gambit.
Center pawn — let's go.
Symmetrical center.
Knight out, pressuring e5.
They defend.
Italian bishop on c4 — targeting f7.
Instead of Bc5, they counterattack your e4 pawn immediately. This is the Two Knights — more aggressive from Black.
You and the bishop are both pointing at f7 now. This is the start of the Fried Liver Attack direction — very sharp.
They have to counterattack! Defending f7 passively loses. This pawn sacrifice is their best shot.
Take it. You're up a pawn and the position is opening up.
They chase your bishop instead of recapturing. Counterattack over defense — that's the spirit of this line.
Check! You gain a tempo while keeping that extra d5 pawn.
They block and attack your bishop. Clever — they'll recapture on d5 next.
Take with the pawn, opening more lines. You're betting on your lead in development.
They recapture and open the b-file for their rook. Both sides have chances — this is razor-sharp chess.
Pull the bishop to safety. The position is wild and double-edged — you'll need to play precisely, but you have the extra pawn.
Center first.
They follow suit.
Knight attacks e5.
They defend.
Italian bishop — aiming at f7.
The Hungarian Defense — they play it safe. No tactical fireworks, just solid. You should punish this passivity.
Strike immediately! They gave you the chance to grab space, so take it.
They support e5 but they're cramped. Your pieces are more active.
Open it up! When you're better developed, opening the position is always good.
They recapture but the position is more open than they wanted.
Trade queens early. Sounds boring? It's not — without queens, your development lead becomes even more dominant.
Their bishop is stuck on d8 now. Awkward.
Develop with tempo. Your pieces are all coming out smoothly while they're tangled up. This is a comfortable edge — well played.
